pattern: Sliding a sorted window of already-visited values behind the current index turns an index-gap constraint into a single binary search per step.
hints: Only pairs that are already at least x indices apart matter, so process the array left to right and only compare the current value against values that are old enough.
Keep every value that is now old enough to be paired with in a structure you can binary search, and grow that structure by exactly one value each time you move forward.

For index j, insert nums[j - x] into a sorted list, then binary-search nums[j] in it; the closest values are the ones immediately before and after the insertion point, so only those two comparisons can matter.

edge_inputs.description: x is zero so every pair of distinct indices is eligible
the closest values sit adjacent to each other
two-element array, the smallest size with a defined pair
x equal to n - 1, only the first and last index can pair
a too-close duplicate is forbidden, but a second duplicate exactly x indices away is legal
values are strictly decreasing so the closest legal pair is not the global closest pair

stress.description: 100k random values with a mid-range x, exercising the sliding sorted structure at scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randrange(1, 10**9 + 1) for _ in range(n)]
    x = rng.randrange(0, n)
    return [nums, x]

stress.description: 100k nearly-sorted values with a small x, so many candidate pairs are legal
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    base = list(range(1, n + 1))
    for _ in range(n // 20):
        i = rng.randrange(0, n)
        j = rng.randrange(0, n)
        base[i], base[j] = base[j], base[i]
    x = rng.randrange(0, min(3, n))
    return [base, x]
